Financial Baseline (Normalized Long-Term Contracts): Headline financials reflect the project's long-term procurement strategy: fixed-price annual contracts for sulfuric acid at the lower tier of the bimodal market, Trammo MOU for anhydrous ammonia, and the LANXESS aqueous ammonia tipping-fee stream.

• Revenue: 407K ST AMS × $411/ST blended realized (granular + grade premiums) + LANXESS tipping fees → $167.5M/yr
• COGS (Raw Materials): Anhydrous NH₃ 97,825 ST × $525/ST + H₂SO₄ 299,210 ST × $133/ST → $91.2M/yr
    Anhydrous volume reduced from 107,860 ST by 10,035 ST NH₃-equivalent from LANXESS aqueous stream (45K ST/yr @ 22.3% NH₃)
• Gross Profit (Revenue − COGS): $76.3M/yr (45.5% gross margin)
• OPEX: Fixed + variable (incl. $0.4M Business Interruption Insurance) → $27.5M/yr
• EBITDA (Gross Profit − OPEX): $48.8M/yr (29.1% EBITDA margin)

Stress Test — Current April 17, 2026 NOLA Spot Market (post-Iran conflict, Hormuz disruption): AMS $435/ST spot, NH₃ $735/ST, H₂SO₄ $285/ST. Most of our feedstock is contractually insulated — sulfuric is fixed-price annual, Trammo MOU ties ammonia to Argus Tampa. At spot pricing without contract insulation, gross margin compresses to ~6% and EBITDA turns briefly negative until feedstock normalizes. Our procurement structure is designed precisely to weather this volatility.
Investment Highlights

Nitrogen Solutions LLC presents a compelling opportunity to invest in a greenfield ammonium sulfate (AMS) production facility at the GlobalPlex Intermodal Terminal, Reserve, Louisiana. With proven GEA crystallization technology, direct Mississippi River access, secured off-take with Ameropa, Trammo MOU for anhydrous ammonia, and the LANXESS aqueous tipping-fee stream, the project delivers a normalized 29.1% EBITDA margin with strong cash-on-cash returns across financing structures in a supply-constrained North American market protected by anti-dumping duties on Chinese AMS.

Strategic Advantages

  • Prime location at Port of South Louisiana
  • Secured feedstock supply through Trammo
  • Long-term off-take agreement with Ameropa
  • GEA process guarantee (50+ installations)
  • Supply deficit market with protective tariffs

Financial Profile

  • $48.8M annual EBITDA (29.1% margin, normalized)
  • $28.7M net income; $39.2M free cash flow (Year 1 stabilized, Deal 1)
  • Debt deals: $25M working capital reserve + $5M min cash floor + PIK toggle
  • 2-year interest-only debt period, then amortization + balloon Yr 10 (Deals 2 & 3)
  • 18-24 month construction period
  • All-equity structure: $128M total cap
  • 30+ year plant design life

Management Team

The team brings 50+ combined years of fertilizer industry experience spanning production, sales, marketing, project development, and EPC contracting.

Scott Vanderventer

Company President & CEO
Age 67  |  Location: Florida
FOUNDER

45-year veteran of the bulk agriculture fertilizer industry. B.S. in Agricultural Economics from Southern Illinois University. Graduate of the Center for Creative Leadership, Colorado Springs.

CF Industries (NYSE: CF) — 25-year tenure as Director of Sales & Marketing. Managed an 11-member national sales organization across multiple product categories, diversified the customer base, and introduced branded product lines. At Matlok Fertilizer (CF subsidiary), directed annual sales exceeding one million tons of commercial fertilizer.

Oxbow Fertilizer — Director of Sales and Global Trader. Improved distribution terminal operations, reducing truck loading costs and enhancing supply availability for wholesale and cooperative customers.

Sul4R-Plus / UlexAndes / Old World Industries — Expanded expertise across sulfur fertilizers, micronutrients, specialty coatings, and global distribution. At Old World Industries, established an entirely new Agriculture Division from inception. Led the alliance with Unity Envirotech, an AMS manufacturer.

Industry Affiliations: Board of Directors, Agricultural Retailers Association (2007-2010); Chairman of the Board, Illinois Fertilizer & Chemical Association (2006).

Christopher Willson

Technical Advisor & Plant Operations Manager
Age 53  |  Location: Alberta, Canada  |  On-site during construction & commissioning
FOUNDER

B.S. in Physics from Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute. 25+ years spanning production operations, capital project engineering, and technology development. Certificate in Business Negotiations from Harvard Law School. Certified Quality Engineer & Auditor (ASQC).

Nitrogen Solutions / NitroGen LLC — Led engineering for Louisiana gasification projects. Designed AMS production plant and sulfuric acid manufacturing facility. Consulting for project closing in the acquisition of Unity Envirotech AMS manufacturing facility.

Eveready Battery Company — 9 years as Senior Quality Assurance Engineer. Led global lithium product quality, developed production processes, advanced air cell line capabilities, and drove continuous improvement programs.

Energy & Project Development — Developer of utility-scale solar projects (40MW & 65MW in South Carolina), landfill gas-to-power conversion (Blue Collar Energy), and portable integrated renewable power systems (MobileGen).

Patents: Seven patents spanning supercritical fluids, bitumen upgrading, ammonia production procedures, hydrogen storage materials, and electrochemical cells.

Dane Revette

Business Manager
Location: Baton Rouge, Louisiana  |  On-site throughout all project phases
MEMBER

30+ years of Louisiana business startup experience. Brings project team building, team management, EPC experience, and deep local engineering and business networks. Key to local hiring and oversight of business functions. On-site throughout construction, commissioning, and operations.

Excel Group — EPC Contractor

20+ years of fertilizer plant construction, maintenance, and operations. Fixed-price GMP contract. Will provide site operations and maintenance under O&M agreement. Subcontract with Audubon Engineering for OSBL design and GEA for ISBL fabrication.

Capstone Partners — Financial Advisor

Project Finance team engaged April 2022 to serve as financial advisor for placing debt and equity capital required to finance construction and initial working capital. Managing the investor process and term sheet negotiations.

Governance & Board of Managers

Board Composition

Scott VanderventerManager, Chairman
Christopher WillsonManager

Board initially consists of two Managers. Vacancies filled by majority vote of Members. Managers may be removed only for gross negligence or willful misconduct, by majority of Members.

Key Governance Provisions

Quorum: Majority of Managers constitutes quorum; affirmative vote of majority present required for Board action.

Member Consent Required: Mergers, sale of substantially all assets, incurrence of debt outside approved budgets, and amendment to Operating Agreement.

Expenditure Threshold: Any single expenditure exceeding $250,000 outside approved budgets requires Member consent.

Indemnification: Company indemnifies Managers and Officers from all claims except those arising from gross negligence or willful misconduct.

Contract Price Calculation Examples (Current Market — April 17, 2026):

1. Ammonia (Trammo MOU):
• Argus Tampa CFR: $775/MT ÷ 1.10231 = $703/ST
• Plus freight: $35/ST (Year 1 base + CPI)
• Plus diesel surcharge: minimal (baseline $3.00/gal)
• Current Contract Price: ~$735/ST | Baseline: $525/ST

2. AMS Granular (Ameropa Off-Take):
• Market average: (Argus $437 + Green Markets $437) ÷ 2 = $435/ST NOLA
• Ameropa discount: 14% minimum
• Current Granular Contract: $435 × 0.86 = $374/ST
• Blended realized (with spray +10%, industrial +15%, enhanced +25% grade premiums): $411/ST

3. Sulfuric Acid (Trammo/Shrieve — Long-Term Fixed-Price Contract):
• Market structure is bimodal: byproduct tier (base-metals / petroleum refining origin) vs. merchant tier (primary sulfur-burning)
• Procurement targets lower byproduct tier — annual fixed-price contract with ±3% annual volatility cap
• Baseline contract: $133/ST (stable lower-tier economics)
• Current Apr 2026 spot for reference: $310/MT CFR ≈ $281/ST FOB, $250–278/ST delivered (Argus); $285/ST mid
• Our contract insulates from current spot volatility
Stress Test Note — Current Market Dynamics:
Current April 2026 NOLA spot pricing reflects acute geopolitical stress: Iran-Israel conflict, US naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, Chinese urea and phosphate export restrictions, and tight Gulf Coast sulfuric acid balance. Our procurement strategy is explicitly designed to avoid this spot exposure: sulfuric is a fixed-price annual contract at the lower bimodal tier, ammonia is indexed via the Trammo MOU with formula smoothing, and LANXESS aqueous offsets ~9.3% of anhydrous demand at a net-negative cost.
Under normalized contracted baseline: Revenue $167.5M − COGS $91.2M = Gross Profit $76.3M (45.5% margin), OPEX $27.5M (incl. BII), EBITDA $48.8M (29.1%). Under hypothetical spot exposure (if contracts were unwound): Revenue $167.5M − COGS $157.2M = Gross Profit $10.3M (6.2% margin), EBITDA $(16.8)M. Headline financials use the contracted baseline.

tipping revenue
Economic benefit Paid $5/ST to accept — LANXESS contracted stream: 45,000 ST/yr @ 22.3% NH₃ = 10,035 ST/yr NH₃-equivalent, reducing anhydrous purchases from Trammo by 9.3%. After 6-stage treatment (see below), integrates into FC crystallizer at ≤20% blend rate (well within capacity). Volume varies ±20% month-to-month based on LANXESS production schedule, but annual nominal is 45,000 ST.
Annual economic value: $225,000/yr tipping-fee revenue + $5.27M avoided anhydrous purchases (10,035 ST NH₃ × $525/ST baseline) = $5.49M/yr total benefit

Contract structure Long-term fixed-price annual contract at lower-tier bimodal market pricing. Price fixed for 12 months with year-over-year volatility capped at ±3% of starting price. Procurement targets the byproduct tier (base-metals / petroleum refining origin) rather than merchant-grade from primary sulfur burning. Baseline $133/ST reflects stable lower-tier contract economics through market cycles. Current Apr 2026 spot ($285/ST, Argus $310/MT CFR) reflects acute tightness — contract insulation avoids this exposure.

Less LANXESS tipping fee revenue (45,000 ST × $5/ST) ($225,000)
TOTAL COGS NET OF TIPPING (Normalized) $90,928,055

Sulfuric Acid Procurement Strategy — Bimodal Market Positioning

The North American sulfuric acid market has a structurally bimodal price distribution. Our procurement strategy exploits this bifurcation to secure stable, lower-tier pricing through fixed-price annual contracts.

Market Tier Source Typical Price Range Volatility Our Exposure
Lower Tier (Byproduct) Base-metals smelting, petroleum refining acid regeneration, oleum producers $100 – $180/ST Moderate — tied to host-operation economics, not sulfur spot ✓ Primary sourcing
(100% of contracted volume)
Upper Tier (Merchant) Dedicated sulfur-burning plants (primary production) $220 – $360/ST+ High — tracks molten sulfur, oil, and shipping markets Not procured
Contract Terms:
• Price fixed for 12 months at contract signing (typically negotiated Q4 for the following calendar year)
• Annual adjustment capped at ±3% of the starting contract price — year-over-year volatility is structurally bounded
• Baseline contract price: $133/ST delivered to Reserve, LA (lower-tier byproduct sourcing)
• Supply redundancy: Trammo and Shrieve both hold positions in lower-tier byproduct aggregation, providing dual-source resilience
• Volume flexibility: ±10% monthly swing allowance within annual contracted volume (299,210 ST/yr)
Why this matters for April 2026: Current merchant-tier spot pricing has spiked to $285–310/ST on acute Gulf Coast tightness driven by Iran conflict disruptions. A plant exposed to spot merchant pricing would face $45M/yr in excess sulfuric acid costs vs. our contracted lower-tier position. The ±3% annual volatility cap means even a sustained upper-tier shock translates to ~$4/ST maximum year-over-year increase in our COGS.

Aqueous Ammonia Stream - Waste-to-Value Opportunity

Economic Model: Industrial waste stream (22.3% NH₃ solution with organics) - Facility receives $5/ST tipping fee to accept and process this material

LANXESS Revenue Stream, Not Cost: Unlike conventional anhydrous ammonia purchases ($525/ST baseline, $735/ST current), the LANXESS arrangement pays the facility paid $5/ST to accept aqueous ammonia waste. After treatment, this becomes feedstock for FC crystallizer.

Economic Impact at 42,000 ST/yr avg (45K cap, ±20% variability): $210,000/yr tipping income + $4.92M reduced anhydrous ammonia purchases (displaces 9,366 ST NH₃ at baseline) + premium "waste-to-value" ESG narrative
Treatment Stage Process Removal Efficiency Output
1. Air Stripping VOC oxidation 50-80% organics Reduced aromatics/anilines
2. Neutralization 2NH₃ + H₂SO₄ → (NH₄)₂SO₄ 100% ammonia AMS solution with trace organics
3. GAC Polishing Granular activated carbon 70-99% aromatics Clean AMS solution
4. FC Crystallization Crystal washing Organics to mother liquor High-purity crystals
5. SAC Polishing Strong acid cation exchange 90-99%+ final cleanup ≤0.01-0.1 ppmw organics
6. DTB Integration ≤20% blend with standard AMS Dilution effect Standard granular product
Overall Reduction Six-stage treatment train ~100,000× (10⁵) Sub-detection level
Triple Economic Advantage:
1. Revenue Generation: $5/ST tipping fee = direct income stream
2. Cost Reduction: Reduces anhydrous ammonia purchases (effective cost = −$5/ST vs. +$525/ST baseline)
3. ESG Premium: Waste-to-value narrative enhances product marketability and potential carbon credit eligibility

Off-Take Agreement Terms
Term Details
Contract Period 5 years from commissioning (target: Dec 2025) with automatic 5-year renewals
Volume Commitment 100% of annual production (~407,000 tons/year granular + specialty grades)
Take-or-Pay Clause Buyer must pay for all production, even if not lifted (subject to storage charges or resale credits)
Pricing Methodology Monthly average of (Profercy NOLA + Argus North America NOLA) ÷ 2, less 14-16% discount
Price Floor Profercy monthly minimum (FOB NOLA) - 14% (if negotiation fails)
Payment Terms 80% within 30 days post-delivery; 20% upon settlement after month-end adjustments
Delivery Terms FOB Reserve, Louisiana - Seller loads to Buyer's transport (truck/barge)
Title Transfer Passes at loading point
Seasonality Adjustments Parties may extend storage or delay shipments to exploit seasonal market highs
Premium Products Spray/industrial/enhanced grades negotiated separately with 10-30% uplifts

Associated Terminals provides loading/unloading services for inbound raw materials (ammonia, sulfuric acid) and outbound AMS product shipments. Rates negotiated based on throughput volumes and service requirements.

De-Risking Strategy — Minor Source Advantage: At 149.8 TPY total criteria pollutants, Nitrogen Solutions qualifies as a Minor Source — eliminating the need for a Title V air operating permit, the 30-day public comment period, and the 45-day EPA review cycle. This streamlines permitting by 3-4 months versus a major source facility. GEA technology has 50+ global installations with proven environmental compliance.

Alternative Site Under Consideration: This tab presents the Bayer Luling / St. Charles Parish site as a potential alternative or secondary development location to the primary GlobalPlex site in Reserve, LA. Infrastructure specifications below are best-estimate pending site visit and data room access — assembled from public sources (news coverage, historical plant records, EPA filings, typical River Road industrial corridor standards). Any firm technical specifications would require site visit, utility availability letters, and direct engagement with Bayer Real Estate / Corporate Development.
Location
Luling, LA
St. Charles Parish, west bank Mississippi River
Distance to New Orleans
~25 miles
Upriver via I-310 / US-90 / River Road
Site History
60+ years
Lion Oil 1954 → Monsanto 1955 → Bayer 2018
Current Operations
Active
~650+ employees (glyphosate/Roundup production)
Site Overview

The Bayer Luling facility is a historic agrochemicals manufacturing complex located on the west bank of the Mississippi River in Luling, St. Charles Parish, Louisiana. Originally commissioned in 1954 as Lion Oil's "Barton Plant" (producing ammonia and chlorine), acquired by Monsanto in 1955, and transferred to Bayer Crop Science upon Bayer's acquisition of Monsanto in 2018. The site has operated continuously for over 60 years as a major chemical manufacturing node within Louisiana's "Chemical Corridor" between New Orleans and Baton Rouge.

Strategic context: In June 2020, Bayer cancelled a $975M dicamba-production expansion at the site (which had already consumed $968M in construction and reached 70% completion). This cancellation left substantial under-utilized infrastructure and land, and Bayer indicated it would "assess options for the plant" over subsequent months. This creates a potential opportunity for greenfield or brownfield tenant development if Bayer is willing to divest, lease, or partner on portions of the site not required for current glyphosate operations.

Why this site is interesting for AMS production:
  • Historical ammonia production: The original Barton Plant produced anhydrous ammonia on-site — suggesting legacy ammonia storage, handling, and safety infrastructure may be present or permittable
  • Sulfuric acid handling experience: Glyphosate production involves phosphorus trichloride and acid chemistry — site is already permitted for corrosive-acid handling under LDEQ and EPA RMP frameworks
  • Mississippi River frontage: Full industrial river access for bulk feedstock (NH₃ barges from Tampa/Donaldsonville; H₂SO₄ from Gulf Coast sources)
  • Existing permits and community relationships: 60+ years of operating in St. Charles Parish, established Title V / RMP compliance posture, community familiarity with chemical industry
  • Under-utilized land and utilities following the cancelled $975M expansion
Infrastructure & Access — Best Estimate Assessment

The following are inferences from public records and typical infrastructure standards for Louisiana River Road chemical industrial sites. Firm specifications require site visit and engagement with Bayer.

Logistics Access

Mode Assessment Confidence Notes
Mississippi River (Dock) Direct river frontage; presumed active dock facility for bulk-chemical barge handling High Historical barge handling of phosphorus trichloride (per EPA RMP filings) confirms deep-water industrial-grade dock. Likely equipped for liquid bulk chemicals (tanker barge unload/load), suitable for NH₃ and H₂SO₄ receipt.
Highway — LA Hwy 18 (River Road) Direct frontage Confirmed Plant entrance confirmed on LA Hwy 18 per local news coverage. Two-lane highway with heavy truck traffic handling.
Highway — US-90 Within 2-3 miles High Major east-west corridor connecting to I-310 and I-10. Full truck access to New Orleans, Baton Rouge, and Gulf Coast destinations.
Highway — I-310 Within 5 miles High Luling Bridge spans Mississippi; I-310 connects to I-10 (east-west) and I-49 north via Baton Rouge.
Rail Access Presumed on-site spur; likely Union Pacific (UP) or Kansas City Southern (KCS) Medium River Road corridor has established rail service from both UP and former KCS (now CPKC). Bayer's glyphosate shipments almost certainly use rail — thus a switching spur or dedicated rail siding is likely present. Capacity and car-spotting capability would need verification.
Airport Access New Orleans Louis Armstrong Intl (MSY): ~20 miles High Comparable to primary GlobalPlex site; supports international technical/engineering travel for commissioning and operations.

Utilities Availability

Utility Assessment Confidence Capacity Estimate
Electric Power Entergy Louisiana — very high-capacity industrial service presumed High Site operates large electrochemical glyphosate production; likely has 69 kV or 138 kV substation on-site or adjacent. Historical Roundup expansion would have required 30+ MW. AMS production ~8-12 MW demand is well within existing capacity.
Natural Gas Industrial-grade service from regional transmission network High Chemical Corridor is crossed by major gas transmission lines (Tennessee Gas, Gulf South, Enable); industrial gas tap presumed to be on-site. Supports steam generation for AMS crystallizer demand.
Process Water Multiple sources — Mississippi River intake, municipal (St. Charles Parish Waterworks), possibly groundwater High Mississippi River intake permit presumed (typical for all River Road chemical sites). Glyphosate production is water-intensive, so on-site treatment and storage are likely substantial. AMS crystallizer demand modest by comparison.
Steam / Cooling Existing site steam system likely; cooling tower capacity likely shared/available Medium Long-operating chemical plants typically have integrated steam/condensate loops and cooling tower banks. Reuse or tap-in of existing infrastructure could significantly reduce new-build utility CAPEX vs. greenfield. Depends on Bayer's willingness to share.
Wastewater Treatment On-site industrial WWTP with Mississippi River discharge permit High Bayer's existing LPDES permit and WWTP capacity could potentially accommodate AMS plant discharge (process condensate, cooling blowdown). Would require LDEQ permit modification.
Compressed Air / Nitrogen Plant air system likely; industrial N₂ tap possible from nearby Air Products / Air Liquide pipeline Medium The Chemical Corridor has industrial gas pipelines serving multiple sites — potential for N₂ tie-in vs. on-site generation.
Emergency Services St. Charles Parish Fire Services, mutual-aid with other chemical plants High Established hazmat response infrastructure across River Road corridor (CHEMTREC, plant mutual aid, LOOP operations).
Regulatory & Permitting Environment
Regulatory Dimension Status / Assessment
Air Permit (LDEQ Title V) Site holds an existing Title V major-source permit for current operations. AMS production as Minor Source (149.8 TPY) would be an incremental addition and likely permittable under existing PSD framework, though new emission units require construction permit.
Water / LPDES Existing Mississippi River intake and discharge permits. Modification for new process streams required but well-understood by LDEQ.
EPA RMP (Risk Management Plan) Site is RMP-covered due to phosphorus trichloride storage. Adding anhydrous ammonia (2-threshold chemical, 10,000 lb RQ) would extend RMP coverage — manageable given existing program maturity.
Zoning & Land Use St. Charles Parish Heavy Industrial (I-2) zoning already in place; no rezoning needed.
Louisiana ITEP (Industrial Tax Exemption) Site historically received significant state incentives: $5M Modernization Tax Credit and $1.7M Economic Development Award Program grant (2016). New greenfield AMS development could potentially qualify for ITEP (property tax abatement) and Quality Jobs Program — Louisiana Economic Development would need to be engaged.
Environmental Risk Site identified in 2023 flood-risk analysis as vulnerable to hurricane storm surge; same Mississippi River flooding exposure as GlobalPlex. Hurricane hardening standards for Louisiana chemical plants apply equally to both sites.
Comparative Assessment — Bayer Luling vs. GlobalPlex (Primary)
Dimension GlobalPlex Reserve (Primary) Bayer Luling (Alternative)
Parish St. John the Baptist St. Charles
River Access ✓ Direct Mississippi frontage; GlobalPlex terminal integrated ✓ Direct Mississippi frontage; legacy industrial dock
Rail Access ✓ Associated Terminals switching; multi-carrier Presumed ✓ (needs verification)
Utilities (Power/Gas/Water) Industrial tenant standards via GlobalPlex ✓✓ Established heavy-industrial infrastructure (30+ MW electrical base)
Site Preparation Cost Standard greenfield ($8.5M in CAPEX) Potentially reduced if brownfield portions of cancelled expansion are reusable
Permitting Speed Minor Source Pending (3–4 months) Potentially faster via modification of existing Title V permit
Community/Political Environment Established industrial tenant relationship with GlobalPlex 60+ year continuous chemical operation; strong ITEP/EDA track record with state
Counterparty GlobalPlex Terminal (commercial lease counterparty — simple) Bayer AG (large multinational — more complex negotiation; potential for JV or preferred-tenant arrangements)
Key Risks Greenfield construction risk (standard) Bayer commitment timeline unknown; Roundup litigation exposure creates corporate uncertainty; site shares hurricane exposure
Available Acreage 14 acres contracted Presumed 20+ acres available within larger Bayer footprint (post-expansion cancellation)
Recommendation: The Bayer Luling site presents a credible alternative that could offer superior utility infrastructure and potentially accelerated permitting via existing Title V modifications. However, it introduces counterparty complexity (Bayer AG corporate development cycles, potential Roundup-litigation-driven portfolio decisions, and unresolved expansion cancellation strategy). Recommended next step: initiate exploratory contact with Bayer Real Estate / Corporate Development to assess openness to tenant or JV arrangements on surplus acreage. The GlobalPlex site remains the primary development path given contract certainty and commercial simplicity.
Key Data Room Items Required (If Bayer Site Advances)

If the Bayer alternative is pursued, the following would be required to convert best-estimate infrastructure assumptions into firm engineering basis:

  1. Site survey and topo — ALTA survey, flood elevation (BFE), soil borings for process equipment foundation design
  2. Utility availability letters — Entergy (electric), regional gas utility, St. Charles Parish Water Works, wastewater
  3. Existing Title V permit and modification pathway — copies of current permit, emission inventory, PSD applicability analysis
  4. EPA RMP filings and facility response plan — to confirm ammonia addition compatibility
  5. Rail spur agreement and siding capacity — direct carrier engagement (UP or CPKC) for switching service terms
  6. Dock specifications and lease terms — dock length, draft, loading arm types, throughput capacity, Bayer's willingness to lease access
  7. Ground lease or purchase terms — from Bayer Real Estate; may involve master services agreement for shared utilities
  8. Environmental Phase I/II (ASTM E1527) — given 60+ year industrial history, baseline environmental conditions must be established and liability allocation negotiated
  9. State/parish incentive package — Louisiana Economic Development engagement for ITEP, Quality Jobs, and modernization credits applicable to new tenant

Selected Option Details
Debt Package Safeguards & Covenants

Debt-bearing structures (Options 2 and 3) are structured as senior term debt with a 2-year interest-only period (Years 3–4 from drawdown), followed by principal amortization through Year 9, and a balloon payment at Year 10 (end of term). The 2-year IO window protects operations during commissioning and early stabilization; amortization then builds equity value over the remaining 5 years; the balloon is typically refinanced at maturity rather than paid from free cash flow. A comprehensive protective covenant package eliminates negative cash flow at the debt-service level and preserves equity holder protection during periods of commodity-market stress.

Key Structural Features (Deals 2 & 3):
• 10-year senior term debt — 2 years interest-only (Years 3–4), then constant-principal amortization at $5M/yr for Years 5–9, balloon of remaining principal at Year 10
• Deal 2 balloon: $66M (of $91M original) | Deal 3 balloon: $58.9M (of $83.9M original) — typically refinanced at maturity
• Working Capital Reserve — equity-funded, ring-fenced on balance sheet at closing (Deal 2: $20M reserve, $59M total equity, $150M project total; Deal 3: $25M reserve, $70.1M total equity, $154M project total)
• $5M Minimum Cash Floor — hard covenant, triggers PIK toggle if threatened
• PIK Interest Toggle — interest converts to principal if cash floor would be breached
• Business Interruption Insurance in G&A ($0.4M/yr premium, $50M coverage)

Debt Service Schedule by Year (Deals 2 & 3)

The 2-year interest-only period provides headroom during operational ramp; amortization begins in Year 5 when steady-state cash flow is established.

Year Phase Deal 2 Debt Service Deal 3 Debt Service Principal Balance (D2 / D3)
1–2 Construction / pre-drawdown $0 $0 $91.0M / $83.9M (not drawn)
3–4 Interest Only (2-year IO period) $7.3M/yr (int) $6.7M/yr (int) $91.0M / $83.9M (unchanged)
5 Amortization begins $12.3M (7.3 int + 5.0 prin) $11.7M (6.7 + 5.0) $86.0M / $78.9M
6 Amortizing $11.9M (6.9 + 5.0) $11.3M (6.3 + 5.0) $81.0M / $73.9M
7 Amortizing $11.5M (6.5 + 5.0) $10.9M (5.9 + 5.0) $76.0M / $68.9M
8 Amortizing $11.1M (6.1 + 5.0) $10.5M (5.5 + 5.0) $71.0M / $63.9M
9 Amortizing $10.7M (5.7 + 5.0) $10.1M (5.1 + 5.0) $66.0M / $58.9M
10 BALLOON (end of term) $71.3M (5.3 int + 66.0 balloon) $63.6M (4.7 + 58.9) $0 (typically refinanced)
TOTAL DEBT SERVICE OVER TERM $143.4M ($52.4M int + $91M prin) $131.5M ($47.6M int + $83.9M prin)
Balloon Refinancing Strategy: At Year 10, cumulative free cash flow (Deal 2: ~$220M+ cumulative FCF post-tax; Deal 3: ~$240M+ cumulative) comfortably supports balloon payment from retained earnings. In practice, the balloon is typically refinanced into a new long-dated facility at lower cost-of-capital (project-proven, de-risked asset commands ~200 bps spread compression). Refinancing preserves equity distributions and avoids large one-time cash outflow.

Cash Flow Waterfall Priority

Available operating cash flow is applied in strict priority order. Debt service sits below operational integrity and the $5M minimum-cash covenant — ensuring the company never funds interest payments by going cash-negative.

Priority Use of Cash Annual (Baseline) Description
1 Operating Expenses $27.5M Labor, utilities, maintenance, property/liability insurance, Business Interruption Insurance ($0.4M), property tax, site lease. Already deducted from EBITDA.
2 Maintenance CapEx $0 – $6.1M Minimal in Years 1–3; $6.1M major turnaround every 4th year (~5% of CAPEX).
3 Working Capital / Inventory Holding ~$0 (steady state) Ramp-up consumes $6M in Year 1; steady state thereafter (30-day AR/inventory/AP cycle).
4 Tax Obligation (25% federal + state) $7.9M – $9.7M Calculated on EBIT less interest (if paid in cash). PIK interest still reduces taxable income.
5 Minimum Free Cash Reserve — $5M FLOOR $5.0M Hard covenant. Company cash balance must never drop below $5M. This floor is replenished before any discretionary debt service.
6 Debt Interest — Cash Pay (if available) $4.7M – $7.3M Paid in cash if all higher priorities are satisfied. Declines as principal amortizes (Years 5+). Otherwise converts to PIK (see toggle below).
7 Scheduled Principal Amortization $0 – $5.0M Years 3–4: $0 (interest-only period). Years 5–9: $5.0M/yr constant principal. Year 10: balloon payment from refinancing.
8 Equity Distributions Residual Last in priority. Paid only from excess cash above $15M "sweep threshold" after all prior obligations.

PIK Interest Toggle Mechanism

Purpose: Eliminate negative cash flow from debt service during periods when operating cash flow is insufficient to pay interest in cash without breaching the $5M minimum-cash covenant.

Trigger Condition: If, in any quarter, (Available Cash for Debt Service) < (Cash Interest Due + $5M Floor Replenishment), then the unpaid portion of interest is converted to Payment-in-Kind (PIK) — accrued to principal rather than paid in cash. PIK accrues at a 200 bps premium to cash interest rate.

Reversion: Cash interest payment resumes in the next quarter where DSCR ≥ 1.25x is restored and the $5M floor is maintained. Accumulated PIK balance is amortized over remaining loan tenor or via cash sweep when DSCR ≥ 2.0x.

Effect: Guarantees the company never borrows short-term debt or draws reserves to pay interest. Debt-service-induced negative cash flow is structurally impossible.

Key Financial Covenants

Covenant Threshold Testing Frequency Consequence of Breach
Minimum Cash Balance $5.0M Continuous (monthly reporting) PIK toggle activates; dividend block
Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR) ≥ 1.25x Quarterly (trailing 4Q) PIK toggle; cash sweep to 100%; no distributions
Cash Sweep Threshold Cash > $15M Annual 50% of excess above $15M prepays debt (until PIK cleared)
Leverage Ratio (Debt/EBITDA) ≤ 4.0x Quarterly Re-price covenant; pricing step-up +50bps
Distribution Test DSCR ≥ 2.0x AND Cash ≥ $15M Prior to each distribution Distribution blocked

Safeguard Verification — Scenario Cash Flow

Metric Normalized
Baseline		 Current Apr 2026
Spot Stress*
EBITDA +$48.8M −$17.2M
Less: Maintenance CapEx $0 $0
Less: Working Capital Change $0 $0
Less: Tax (on EBIT net of interest) $7.9M $0 (loss)
Cash Available for Debt Service +$40.9M −$17.2M
Deal 2: Interest Due ($7.3M) Cash Pay ✓
DSCR = 5.6x		 PIK Toggle + WC Reserve Draw ▲
$20M reserve absorbs 11-14 months stress
Deal 3: Interest Due ($6.7M) Cash Pay ✓
DSCR = 6.1x		 PIK Toggle + WC Reserve Draw ▲
$25M reserve absorbs 14-18 months stress
$5M Cash Floor Maintained? YES ✓ (comfortable) YES ✓ (by PIK toggle)
Debt-Service-Induced Cash Burn? NONE ✓ NONE ✓ (eliminated by PIK)

*Spot stress scenario assumes all procurement contracts (sulfuric fixed-price, Trammo MOU, LANXESS offset) are simultaneously unwound to spot-market exposure. This is a hypothetical worst-case — our contract architecture is specifically designed to prevent this exposure.

✓ CONFIRMED: Debt Package Eliminates Negative Cash Flow from Debt Service

Under the normalized baseline, all three deal structures service debt comfortably in cash with DSCR of 5.7x–6.1x, maintaining the $5M floor with $30M+ buffer. Under extreme spot-stress, the PIK toggle structurally prevents the company from going cash-negative on debt service — interest defers to principal rather than consuming operating cash or the minimum reserve. The company will never borrow short-term working capital to pay interest, and the $5M floor is never breached.
✓ MITIGATIONS NOW EMBEDDED IN DEAL STRUCTURE (Deals 2 & 3):

Following the residual-risk analysis, the debt-bearing deal structures now embed two additional protections against sustained operating stress, in addition to the PIK toggle and $5M floor:
  • Working Capital Reserve — equity-funded protected cash, held on balance sheet separately from operating cash. Total project costs: Deal 2 $150M (includes $20M reserve), Deal 3 $154M (includes $25M reserve). Sized to cover 11–18 months of stressed operations. Draw only permitted upon covenant breach and with lender consent.
  • Business Interruption Insurance — $0.4M/yr premium (~0.8% of $50M coverage), included in G&A OPEX line. Covers force-majeure-triggered feedstock price spikes and extended plant outages.
Commodity hedging overlay (ISDA collar on ammonia exposure) remains under evaluation but is not modeled at this level — Trammo MOU formula smoothing plus the above reserves are judged sufficient for current predevelopment-stage analysis.
⚑ RESIDUAL RISK FLAG (Substantially Mitigated):

With the WC reserves in place, the hypothetical spot-stress scenario (EBITDA −$16.8M) would consume approximately $17M of reserve in Year 1, leaving headroom plus the ongoing PIK toggle protection on debt service. Combined with BII insurance coverage, Deal 2 ($20M reserve) can absorb approximately 11–14 months of full stress exposure, and Deal 3 ($25M reserve) can absorb approximately 14–18 months, without equity impairment or debt-service default.

What this does not protect against: A sustained (2+ year) simultaneous unwind of all three procurement contracts (fixed-price sulfuric, Trammo MOU, LANXESS stream). This is a very-low-probability tail event given contract diversification and counterparty quality, but it is not zero. If investors require absolute protection, the optional commodity hedging overlay should be added as a fourth protective layer at ~0.5–1% of revenue annually.

Deal 1 (InfraNext all-equity) does not include the $25M WC reserve. InfraNext as majority equity holder (97%) bears residual operating risk directly; the structure relies on InfraNext's institutional balance sheet rather than a dedicated reserve.

Environmental Performance Summary

Nitrogen Solutions facility operates 44-68% cleaner than conventional AMS plants through: waste-to-value ammonia pathway, no-combustion sulfuric acid process, closed-loop water recycling, and best-available scrubber technology. Total GHG emissions: 0.21 kg CO₂e/kg AMS vs. industry average 0.38 kg CO₂e/kg AMS (45% reduction).

Pollutant Nitrogen Solutions
(TPY)		 Standard AMS Plant*
(TPY)		 Reduction Technology Advantage
PM₁₀ (Particulate Matter) 87.6 165.0 ▼ 47% High-efficiency wet scrubbers (>99% capture)
PM2.5 (Fine Particulate) 58.4 110.0 ▼ 47% Advanced scrubber design with mist eliminators
NOₓ (Nitrogen Oxides) 3.8 12.5 ▼ 70% Low-NOₓ burners, natural gas (vs. heavy oil)
SO₂ (Sulfur Dioxide) 0.0 45.0 ▼ 100% Non-combustion sulfuric acid (vs. sulfur burning)
VOC (Volatile Organics) 0.0 2.8 ▼ 100% No organic materials in process
CO (Carbon Monoxide) 0.0 8.2 ▼ 100% Efficient natural gas combustion
TOTAL CRITERIA POLLUTANTS 149.8 343.5 ▼ 56% Best-available technology

Key Advantage: Waste-derived sulfuric acid (vs. virgin sulfur burning) saves 62,000 tonnes CO₂e/year alone. Standard plants emit 0.21 kg CO₂e/kg H₂SO₄ from sulfur combustion; waste-acid pathway: 0.025 kg CO₂e/kg (88% reduction).
Water Usage & Discharge
Water Stream Nitrogen Solutions Standard AMS Plant Environmental Benefit
Freshwater Intake 12,500 GPM (peak) 18,000 GPM ▼ 31% reduction
Process Water Recycling Rate 85% 60% Closed-loop design
Wastewater Discharge (LPDES) 0 GPD 2,400 GPD Zero liquid discharge
Stormwater Management 100% contained Varies Oil/water separators, retention
Water Treatment Systems:
  • Cooling Tower Blowdown: Recycled to crystallizer mother liquor (zero discharge)
  • Scrubber Blowdown: Neutralized and recycled to process (ammonium content recovered)
  • Condensate Recovery: Steam condensate returned to boiler feed (closed loop)
  • Rainwater: Collected in retention basin, oil/water separation, discharge via LPDES (prepared)
Solid Waste & Ground Protection
Waste Stream Annual Volume Disposal Method RCRA Classification
Hazardous Waste <500 kg/yr Licensed RCRA facility CESQG (Conditionally Exempt)
Lab Samples & QC Waste ~200 kg/yr Chemical waste contractor Lab pack disposal
Maintenance Solvents/Oils ~300 kg/yr Recycling where possible Universal waste
Non-Hazardous Industrial Waste ~50 tonnes/yr Landfill or recycling Non-hazardous solid waste
Filter Media/Spent Carbon ~25 tonnes/yr Industrial landfill Inert solid waste
Packaging/Drums/Scrap ~25 tonnes/yr Metal recycling Recyclable materials
Spill Prevention & Ground Protection:
  • Secondary Containment: All chemical storage tanks in 110% containment basins
  • Concrete Pad: Entire process area on sealed concrete with spill drains to containment
  • SPCC Plan: Spill Prevention, Control, and Countermeasure plan (40 CFR 112)
  • Emergency Response: Spill kits, trained personnel, automatic shut-off systems
  • Groundwater Monitoring: Not required (no underground storage tanks, sealed surfaces)
